Off Calendar Oct-01-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA PACIFIC MEDICAL GROUP, INC. d.b.a. Brown & Toland Physicians' Motion To Seal is granted. The Rosenberg-Wohl declaration filed on August 24, 2020 in support of plaintiff's reply brief for the motion to compel production is sealed. California Rules of Court, Rule 2.550(a)(3) expressly provides that the rules regarding sealed records (i.e., Rule 2.550 and Rule 2.551) "do not apply to discovery motions and records filed or lodged in connection with discovery motions or proceedings." Defendant is therefore entitled to have the declaration sealed. None of plaintiff's arguments has any merit and he totally misreads the sealing rules. Defendant will still need to comply with the sealing rules if the cited information is used to adjudicate the merits of the parties' dispute and the parties' designation of documents to be confidential also do not override the sealing rules.

Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar May-28-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES VIA COURTCALL: Atty. DAVID ROSENBERG-WOHL of Hershenson Rosenberg-Wohl (Tel 415-829-4330) for plaintiff;
Atty. MICHAEL A. GAWLEY of Kessenick Gamma (Tel 415-362-9400) for defendant CA Pacific Medical Group;
Atty. LAURA C. HURTADO of Pillsbury Winthrop (Tel 415-983-1082) for defendants Kelly Robison, et al.

HEARING RE DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA PACIFIC MEDICAL GROUP, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED.

Matter argued and submitted. Court adopts its tentative ruling: Defendant California Pacific Medical Group, Inc. d.b.a. Brown & Toland Physicians' demurrer to plaintiff Dan Kelly's first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled in its entirety. Liberally construing the complaint, plaintiff adequately pleads a cause of action for breach of an express or implied-in-fact contract. (See C.A. v. William S. Hart Union High School Dist. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 861, 872 ["To survive a demurrer, the complaint need only allege facts sufficient to state a cause of action; each evidentiary fact that might eventually form part of the plaintiff's proof need not be alleged."]; see also Quelimane Co. v. Stewart Title Guaranty Co. (1998) 19 Cal.4th 26, 38 ["If the complaint states a cause of action under any theory, regardless of the title under which the factual basis for relief is stated, that aspect of the complaint is good against a demurrer."].) The FAC pleads a cause of action for breach of an express or implied-in-fact contract arising from Brown & Toland's Bylaw provision requiring good cause for termination of a director. (FAC 66 ["Article IV, Section 7(b) permits directors to remove fellow directors 'for cause' if that director violates Brown & Toland's 'Conflict of Interest Policy, Confidentiality Policy, Code of Conduct for Directors, or any other applicable policy.'"].) Additionally, the FAC pleads a cause of action for breach of the express or implied term that such a removal may take place only after the affected director is given notice and an opportunity to address the Board of Directors. (FAC 67, 77-78.) Plaintiff alleges he received no notice of his proposed removal and no opportunity to discuss his removal. (FAC 68-70.) (See Gomez v. Lincare, Inc. (2009) 173 Cal.App.4th 508, 520 [trial court erred in sustaining demurrer to cause of action for breach of express contract to pay plaintiffs overtime wages where plaintiffs alleged terms of contracts were communicated orally and in writing and were memorialized in employee handbook and in plaintiffs' paychecks]; Stillwell v. The Salvation Army (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 360, 380 ["'Generally, the existence of an implied-in-fact contract requiring good cause for termination is a question of fact for the trier of fact.'"]; Race v. Bally Technologies, Inc. (N.D. Cal. Nov. 25, 2008), 2008 WL 5047700, at *4-*5 [plaintiff stated claim for breach of contract to compensate him as director in accord with board resolutions].) The third cause of action, for breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, rises and falls with the existence of a contract between the two parties. (See Pacific States Enterprises, Inc. v. City of Coachella (1993) 13 Cal.App.4th 1414, 1425.) As discussed above, the FAC alleges sufficient facts to plead a breach of contract claim, thus the demurrer to the third cause of action is overruled. On the court's own motion, paragraphs 71, 72, and 73 are stricken from the FAC. (Code Civ. Proc., 436; Lodi v. Lodi (1985) 173 Cal.App.3d 628, 631.) In the court's January 27, 2020 order, the court sustained defendant's demurrer to the cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty without leave to amend on the ground that a corporate director owes a fiduciary duty to the corporation and shareholders, not other way around. In addition, the individual directors are no longer named defendants in the case. Order signed.
